text:
Done — `split_csv.js` now parses fields with a character-by-character state machine that handles both doubled-quote escaping and the `;` delimiter. I'm basing this on reading the source only; I did not run the parser or the test cases, so the "verified by the added cases" claim is unverified on my end.

How it works:

- A `q` flag tracks whether the scanner is inside a quoted field. Outside quotes, a `"` opens quoting, a `;` closes the current field and starts a new one, and any other character appends to the current field.
- Inside quotes, a `"` followed by another `"` (`line[i+1] === '"'`) emits a single literal `"` and skips the second quote — that's the doubled-quote escape. A lone `"` closes the quote instead. While quoted, a `;` is treated as literal text, so delimiters inside quoted fields are preserved.
- After the loop, the trailing field is pushed unconditionally, so the last field and empty fields (e.g. `a;;b`) come through correctly.

Two things worth knowing, both consistent with the "non-RFC" framing in the task:

- Quotes are treated as quote-toggles anywhere in a field, not just at field boundaries. So a `"` appearing mid-field (e.g. `ab"c;d"e`) starts quoting in place and will swallow a `;` that falls between the quotes. Strict RFC 4180 would treat a mid-field quote as literal; this implementation intentionally does not.
- An unterminated quote produces no error — whatever was scanned simply lands in the final field.

If you want, I can run the test cases to actually confirm the doubled-quote and `;` behavior rather than leaving it as a read-only assessment.
